entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Okay. So if you can start by introducing yourself and the name of the case, the farm, the project, and a bit of background as well.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Okay. Yeah. My name is Sarah Stuckey. This farm is Finca Laguna Verde.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Can we start again?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Just. Okay.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: That's okay. That's okay.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Okay, let's start. Good.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Okay. So my name is Sarah Stuckey. This, our farm, Finca Laguna Verde, is located in Monteverde, Costa Rica, and we have been doing regenerative agriculture for the past seven years. We have started out mostly as a dairy farm and we are now diversifying into coffee gardens, tilapia, rabbits and fruit trees.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Great. Can you talk a bit about the transition process from the historical model to the model you're applying?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: The transition process started with completely eliminating chemical fertilizers, also ivermectin, and decreasing the amount of inputs, mostly grains concentrates, and also changing the grazing system, increasing the amount of rest period in the pastures, coupling that with soil analysis, organic matter, carbon and nutrient analysis in the soil, and organic, more organic practices. We are not an organic farm, but we are slowly trying to implement more natural and holistic practices, eliminating chemicals and also certification for carbon neutral.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: You've given me this statistic before, but I don't really bought it in the data. I think it'd be great to have it in the video. Can you talk about your carbon sequestration? Lots of things there.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Yes, I do have figures in terms of the carbon sequestration. We have gone from being carbon positive. In other words, we have gone from being a net carbon emitter emitting methane gas from the cows to carbon negative. That means that we are currently sequestering 302 tons more carbon into our soils than the greenhouse gases that our cows are emitting.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Fantastic. Thank you. What products and services do you offer and what do you contribute to the food system?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Finca Laguna Verde offers dairy. We sell to the local cheese factory. That is our main product. We also produce organic coffee that is sold locally. We produce organic garden products. Those are for self consumption, but they also go to a local restaurant. And we are starting to diversify into some tilapia and some rabbits. And so far those have only been self consumption, although we hope solely to expand those and also be marketing them locally.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Okay, thanks so much. What would you like to achieve?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Oh, what we would like to achieve. That's an excellent question. We want to really become more resilient for climate. One of our goals is to build back the ecosystem as much as possible so that we can increase our food production, increase the amount of biodiversity on the farm and really start to have an ecosystem that can withstand some of the extremes that climate change is starting to provide or to show us impacts on our local community. So we want our soil to be able to hold more water, we want to have more biodiversity, we want to be able to be more resilient.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: What obstacles have you faced in the transition process and how have you overcome them?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: In our transition process to regenerative agriculture, we have faced obstacles in terms of peer pressure. People don't want to change or don't accept that you can change the way you do things. We have also encountered obstacles in terms of pricing of our products. Selling to a cheese factory means that our milk is priced at the same price that everyone who is not doing regenerative agriculture is receiving. That makes it different. Difficult obstacles also include increased costs, especially labor costs. Moving to regenerative agriculture is more work. It is more work. And so that has been an obstacle. And also learning. This is a relatively new field. There is information out there, but each farm is its own ecosystem. And so you really have to learn about what works for you and what doesn't. And what works for your neighbor might not work for you. So. So it's really changing your mindset. To start to have a production system that's based on the ecosystem, you cannot follow recipes. You have to really be adaptive and adapt to your ecosystem, adapt to your current context and climate, which is always changing. And so that's a big challenge. Did I get all the question or did I miss?

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: That's perfect. Who do you work with and who do you collaborate with?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: We work with, I guess you could say we work with the cheese factory. In terms of our clients, the collaboration happens more at a level of the people who work with us, our workers that work on the farm. Regenerative agriculture is a process of constantly learning. So you have to really rely on all of your people that are working within the system to provide information on a constantly changing environment. You have to collaborate with the Ministry of Agriculture. You have to collaborate with your clients in terms of. As we slowly diversify our farm, we have to really try to figure out who. Who are our clients, who's willing to support this type of food system.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Can you mention also the collaboration with the scientific community?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Oh, yes, thank you very much. Here on the farm, we also collaborate with biologists. We have long term studies happening on this farm in terms of number of bird counts. Also long term study for impacts of climate change on our community, documenting the changes in species diversity that's happening. We have documented altitudinal migration of species thanks to the impact of climate change. We also collaborate with educational organizations, Monteverde Institute, cie, local universities that want to come visit our farm, and we allow them also to do some studies and research. But we also help educate them in the impacts of climate change and regenerative agriculture and the changes that need to happen in our food system.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Great. Last question. What's your advice to people who want to do the same thing?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Oh, what's my advice to people who want to do the same thing? That's an excellent question. I think that I would say to people that want to do regenerative agriculture that it is possible. It's fun, it's exciting. You're going to embark on a learning curve that you never imagined. You're going to learn about the ecosystem, you're going to learn about the soil, you're going to learn about change the way you think. It's not going to be easy. There are challenges, but there are also many rewards. And regenerative agriculture really presents an opportunity for increasing the nutrient value of our food, for becoming more resilient for climate change, for building back our soil, for bringing food production back into our communities and increasing our health.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Thanks so much.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: All right. Can we. Testing. Does it work? Okay, good. Okay.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Okay, cool. So I've given you a bit of an introduction about the research. You've told me a lot about the project.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: In advance of this interview. So at some point you might repeat.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Yourself, but that's no worries.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: The process. Okay, just to begin, if you can just tell us your name, the date.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Okay. My name is Sarah Stuckey, and today is December 11, 2024.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Okay. So discussion guide. So the main thing, I mean, just as a beginning, if you can give a sort of brief introduction to the project, just an overview.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Who you are, your role in it, sort of where you come from, where you're going, who's involved, what motivates you. Just a brief introduction.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Okay. I manage our family dairy farm. This farm started production back in 1957 and has slowly grown over the years to become a conventional dairy farm. I took over the management of this farm eight years ago, and for one year, I operated it under a conventional model and then realized that that model was not sustainable, both economically and from a climate or point of view in terms of carbon emissions being a dairy farm, that carbon emissions are the CO2 and greenhouse gas emissions are something we felt like we needed to contribute and do our piece and lower our emissions, especially the methane piece. So we looked at our whole operation and decided that, no, we were going to do everything we could to become carbon neutral. And that led us down the path of regenerative agriculture, completely changing our input system in terms of eliminating chemical fertilizer, reducing the outside inputs, changing our grazing system to have first more intense rotational grazing and now more adaptive grazing, and becoming enrolling in Costa Rica's NAMA program, which is nationally appropriate mitigation actions, becoming certified carbon neutral under that. And so that is basically the path that we've gone down the past seven years. We are a small farm. We milk approximately 30 cows at any one time. And that presents challenges in terms of your options, in terms of your market and what you can do. So we've slowly. We're slowly diversifying and transitioning to other crops. One, from the sustainability part, financial sustainability, but also two, from the regenerative point of view, in terms of adding more diversity into the farm is making us more resilient and more regenerative.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Thank you. Okay, so first I want to get to know the project itself in sort of practical terms. So who's involved? How did you come to be involved? You and who else is working, directing on the farm?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Okay. The farm was started. Well, my parents Bought this farm in 1980 even though it's been a dairy farm since the early 50s. But. And my parents managed it up until eight years ago. Right now, currently in the farm I am the active manager. My father sometimes weighs in on some subjects but. But most of the active administration is up to me. See, see? Can we pause a second? Sorry about that. Happens when you multitask.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: You were telling me about your reactive manager of farm.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: So the farm has basically three pretty full time workers and two part time workers. Besides the dairy, we have about a hectare of coffee. We have garden organic gardens, we have tilapia, we have rabbits, we have honeybees. And we're currently thinking and working about how we can continue to further diversify. Oh, and we also have fruit trees. So there are several aspects that we've incorporated into the regenerative process. Does that fully cover the question?

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Well, just. It's a conversation.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Okay. All right.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: So it'd be useful to know sort of the size of the farm. You've mentioned how many employees there are.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: The farm is approximately 80 hectares. The productive part of that is actually only about 20. A good part of the farm is cliff edge which cannot be pretty steep. Cr, as you know, straight up and down is not exactly farmable. And then another part of it is woods which we have intentionally left. So basically the, the system is set up in a series of 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 different groups of pastures with woods that act as natural wind breaks between them. And that's also important to maintain the biological corridors and the diversity of the. In the balance of the ecosystem.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: How many pastures do you have?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Okay. When we switched to regenerative agriculture, well before we switched to regenerative agriculture, we had about 60 pastures and we would do approximately 30 day rotations with more or less 30 day pastures and 30 night pastures, more or less. But the pastures were all different sizes. And because we were more an extractive depletive model using chemical fertilizers and whatnot, even some of the larger pastures couldn't sustain the herd. So when we made the switch seven years ago to regenerative agriculture, we measured the whole farm using a drone and a gps. And then we did calculations based on carrying capacity and we basically reconfigured and redrew the number of pastures. So now we currently have approximately 160 pastures. We've basically made them all a lot smaller so our rotation now is closer. It varies a lot depending on the time of year because we have a very intense dry season and then the wet season is Opposite. And so the production of and quality of forage is pretty variable throughout the year. But our rotation goes anywhere from 55 to 75 days. It's generally longer in the rainy season and it's shorter in the dry season because in the dry season there's a lot less capacity for the grass to recover as quickly.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Did you feel that it's. The balance is great now.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: It's much better. I guess using the word correct is difficult because when you're doing regenerative agriculture there are really no recipes. You can't say it's always going to be this because the ecosystem is a complex, changing, evolving ecosystem. And so I would say for where I am right now, it's pretty good. But where I am right now may not be where I need to be in six months or a year from now. Because regenerative agriculture really, you have to work based on the ecosystem and not on a set defined rotation. When you get into following a set recipe is when you start to run into troubles and you start to end up again with an extractive depleted model. Because that's not the way nature works.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Okay, can you explain the legal structure of the farm and the relationships that are involved with governance or institutions or patient or whatever else?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Okay, legally the farm operates as a corporation. In Costa Rica we call those Socia Nonimas. And so, and that's just the way we've, we've decided to do business. So it's set up that way, but it's a completely family owned corporation. I mean, so it's not like we have outside investors or anything like that. It's just the family. So it's a small family run farm. And the decision to run that way had more to do with just legal contracts and the tax structure in Costa Rica and just how we wanted to do business. So that's, that's how it's set up.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: And are there any, you know, what's the sort of regulatory oversight or is there any or relationship with governance or influences how you go about your business?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Well, obviously we're, the governance is, is limited or, or constrained by Costa Rica's legal tax system. Right? You mean there's certain things you have to do to be compliant and, and to operate legally as a business in terms of, and I'm not quite sure if your question means like in terms of the governments in terms of regenerative agriculture or it means in terms of just how we operate on a day to day basis from a legal standpoint.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: An example could be, for example, if there are Any welfare standards for the animals that you have to. Or.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Okay, I see what you practices. Okay, yeah, that's an important question and thanks for clarifying. I would say that. Okay, it's important to understand right now that we're selling all of our milk to the Monteverde cheese factory, which is operated under a transnational. That transnational is called Sigma. And Sigma is a company that is certified at different levels for different things. They have haccp, they have different certifications that they require for to be able to export and to sell at large supermarket chains. For example, Walmart, which is an important market in Costa Rica. So we have to adhere to different quality standards in our milk. Certain temperature regulations, certain regulations for infrastructure, certain regulations for antibiotics or. Well, basically we're not allowed to send any milk to the cheese factory that has any sort of antibiotic residue or medicines, any trace elements. Yes, we test for that and we follow strict protocols for cleanliness. We were monitored in terms of bacteria and somatic cell content, fat content, protein content, solid content for the milk. And the company is currently being certified for animal wellness. We have fulfilled all of the necessary regulations or coursework for that. And I would expect that certification to be coming out within the next, I would say max two to three months. They said they were going to be doing all the verification in November, which is now passed. So it's in the works and it's coming. Besides that, we also have Bandera Sule Colojica, which is a Costa Rican Blue flag, Blue flag ecological certification that monitors. It's a voluntary certification, but it basically monitors the aspects of animal welfare. It monitors water usage, usage of things like fossil fuels maintenance and equipment resilience, impact of climate change, soil usage and different things. And so in order to be certified, which we are under that there's a series of reports that have to be filed and things that we have to do to document the things that we're doing on the farm. And we have been certified under that for, oh, gee, gosh, five years. I think it's five. I think we're for the fifth. I'm about to submit the report for the sixth year.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Congratulations. Do you have any other sort of achievements or awards or anything like this?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Generally for we. We are often given a recognition from the cheese factory for quality of milk. So that low bacteria count, low somatic cell counts and things like that? I would guess, I would say that's, I guess one of our main other distinctions. So that. And the carbon neutral, which is something that we've worked very hard to get. And then there are other types of certifications that are also required for sending the milk to the factory. For example, be free of brucellosis, tuberculosis. And we have to have a veterinary certificate of operation as well.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Congratulations on all that. Sounds like quite some achievements and a transformation in the direction of the farm. Let's talk a bit more about sort of practicalities that they're running at the farm. So who's doing what sort of, how do the operations function in a practical sense, you know, who, who's involved and what sort of activities.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Okay, well I guess from a daily aspect point of view we have one main milker then and we have one main person, actually two main people that work in the fields pretty much full time. One of them covers the milker on his day off because you have to milk cows 365 days a year, 366 on leap year, no break for birthdays or Christmas or anything else. So that alone requires a minimum of two people. But we have another person which is my husband that also is in charge of a lot of projects on the farm. Fence, repairing fences, looking at water. I mean on, on a farm there is always something that is happening. The water goes out and you have to fix it immediately because the cows need to drink and, and or there's an issue with electric fences and the cows get out or a tree falls down on a fence. I mean there's all sorts of day to day tasks that consume your day. So those, the, the milker is in charge of milking and then the two field people are mostly out in the field, the healing and they provide some support to the milker. For example, there's, there's moments when we, the vet comes or we have to do a vaccination or a cow gets sick and then they're kind of on call to provide support to the milker as needed besides doing all the jobs out in the field. Then we have one part time person who is mostly in charge of the gardening and, and, and managing the organic gardens. And the other part time person gives some support to the milker in terms of complying with regulations at the barn level. Cleanliness, hygiene. Because when you milk cows twice a day that means they go into the barn and they poop twice a day. And from the requirements of the hygiene and plant requirements of what they expect and what's called the cvo, the Veterinary Certificate of Operation, we have to follow certain protocols for bond, cleanliness, for infrastructure. So the other part time person provides support with that. I am, I Guess you could kind of categorize me as the general manager. I do a lot of coordination of jobs. I do a lot of the book work, I do a lot of the paperwork, the administration and all the banking, all, all those fun things that eat up your day. And also I'll make sure that we're compliant with regulations, certifications and everything we need to do so that we can stay in operations as well as kind of designing the strategic part of where we're going and what we're doing and how we're going to improve on the farm and how we're going to continue down this regenerative path.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Yeah. How do you make those decisions?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: A lot of it is from the regenerative aspect. A lot of it's observation looking. I mean the regenerative agriculture is really a nature based agriculture. Like I mentioned earlier, it's not a recipe based. So for example, yesterday I spent an hour and a half walking all over the farm, looking at the fields, looking at what my plant diversity was, seeing how much grass was in the fields, seeing what the damage from all the rain that we've had, how the roads are doing, thinking about changes that need to happen. Okay, where do we need to put in a windbreak or what changes need to be made here? Are the cows being grazed properly in the fields? Because a lot of regenerative agriculture and making sure you have enough pasture to get through the dry season means you really have to watch your levels of grazing in the pastures. And then that involves, okay, working with the men and making suggestions. Well, this aspect of the farm is ready or we have to be careful. We can't over graze in this area. So there's a lot of its observation going and looking at those different aspects.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: And then sort of structurally, if you decide that you want to make a change or that something needs to be done differently. Who's involved in the consensus?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Well, we, we, we have weekly staff meetings where we discuss things and we discuss options and projects and gather ideas and ultimately I am responsible for most of the decisions. If I need my dad's input, I will. But ultimately we, we listen to what the concerns workers are, gather ideas, try to, to generate decisions based on what everyone wants or what people think work best. But ultimately I'm for good or for bad the decision maker.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Okay, and then what's the, the relationship of the workforce? So you mentioned your husband and you've got some other guys, you know, how did you find them, recruit them? What sort of, of contracting do you have?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: We obviously follow all Costa Rican labor regulations. And, and there's certain, and I mention that because there's certain protocols legally you have to follow even if you're, if you're for recruiting workers, for example, you can't have gender discrimination. You have to advertise certain ways. You have to. There are certain legal requirements in terms of contracts and minimum wage. And we follow all of that. The reality is, and I see this even at a global level, and it's a big concern I have, people don't want to work on farms, period. I just got back from a regenerative agriculture conference in Madison, Wisconsin and some of the speakers I asked that question, I said, how are you dealing with labor issues? Because it's an issue in Costa Rica and somebody from North Dakota said it's a big problem and I'm getting my workers from South Africa. And so this is a global problem. And I think for Monteverde it's perhaps amplified because we live in an area where there's tourism, where there's economic opportunity and people would rather go be a guide on a canopy tour or a bartender or waitress or whatever rather than come and milk cows. And, and so labor is a huge challenge. And the reality is that we live in an area that geographically doesn't allow for really mechanization or anything like that. And even if it did, going down the regenerative path that we are, that complicates it. I mean, there are some things we could do a little bit more mechanically, but it's hard to drive a tractor on these hillsides. Right? It's just not very practical. I could drive it in a very small area, but it's not practical. Whereas if I was in the middle of, I don't know, Kansas, where it's nice and flat, it would be a whole different aspect. So there's some parts of regenerative agriculture that are hard to implement. I mean when you, it's not like you can easily go out and seed cover crops for plant diversity when your terrain doesn't allow for, for tractors. And it's not impossible to get the seed diversity, but it requires a system where you're more labor dependent. And we live in an area where it's difficult to get late to get labor. And so right now we're lucky that we have two full time hired laborers. They're both Costa Rican. Right now our part time laborers are also Costa Rican. In the past we have had to rely on immigrant labors. But again, following all the labor laws and requirements and making sure people have work permits and so forth and so on.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: How then, I'm curious to know, has your labor requirement changed since you've moved from conventional to regenerative development? Agriculture? What does that look like?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: It has increased some and part of that is due to the diversification on the farm. If we were just milking cows, we could probably get by with just two workers. But when you add a garden, when you add bees, when you add coffee, all of a sudden you require a lot more labor. Right now the coffee picking season is coming up soon and it's going to be a challenge to find who can pick that crop. And that's, that's. I don't. I mean, we're gonna have to figure it out because it's a temporary job. It's almost something for a little while. It's a time of year when the weather's generally rainy and wet and no one wants to go out and be wet all day. And it's high tourist season and so it's, it's difficult. So, yeah, in the regenerative system, our labor demands have increased and it's going to be. It's a challenge. And every day it's more of a challenge.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: You mentioned a couple of the obstacles and challenges that you faced and overcoming or something you have succeeded at. Are there other notable obstacles and challenges that you face and overcome or that you see ahead that you need to tackle?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: I would say one, there's a couple. There's some other very important challenges. One is in terms of regulations and governments at a state level, a lot of the things they require of us are more geared to producing a product conventionally. And some of this legislation is put in practice from either a fiscal point of view, in terms of somebody in the national legislature said, yeah, let's make farmers do this with no thought to what being a farmer actually entails. And, and it's, it's, it's time consuming for the farmers. And a perfect example of that, of something that's going into regulation very, very shortly. The law was passed about a year ago and people had about 18 months to implement. It was. Is an electronic ear tag system for all your cows where you have to have. A government person is the only one authorized to install the electronic ear tag. And then any type of movement with your cow or anything you do with them is going to have to be uploaded electronically onto a government webpage. And what the government's saying is, well, this will, this, this will create traceability of your animals, which I would say might be important. If Costa Rica were a huge Beef exporter. The reality is we're not, I mean beef exporter I would think of as United States, Argentina. Costa Rica is not. But that's their excuse. And this is, this is, these are regulations that have been put in place from a legislative perspective of well, yeah, let's make our country accessible to export beef to Europe. The reality is the percentage of beef that might potentially even go to Europe is minuscule, if any. It's not exactly. We're not a big beef exporting country. So those types of regulations just make it harder to do business if you're a small farmer. If, if I was a farm that was running 5,000 cows and, or a CAFO feedlot or whatever, yeah, that might make more sense because then if there was a health issue, you would trace that animal back and find a problem. Or if you have an outbreak, I mean, perfect example like in the States now is the big thing is the whole bird flu type thing. Like if I was running in that type of an operation, then you could say, okay, something like that might make sense. I'm milking 30 cows. I have a total of 60, 65 animals on my farm. I know exactly when or if one of them is sick or I know exactly the origin of them and I know their mothers and their grandmothers. Like it doesn't make sense for a small farmer to have to comply with those regulations. So the regulations and the environment under which we do things is, is a limiting factor because it's designed for a globalized conventional market and that's not who we are. And, and, and the other, the other limitation is, and it's kind of related to that is Costa Rica back in the 1980s made a conscious decision to adopt neoliberal policies that have really been detrimental to small farmers. And Costa Rica went from being largely self sufficient and having good food security to. Now I haven't looked at the data in the last six months, but I know at least 75% of Costa Rica's food is imported now. And basically people aren't producing food anymore. So Costa Rica now has a heavy dependence on imported food. All of the policies since the mid-1980s have been to defund the Ministry of Agriculture, which provides some support to farmers. And every year there are budget cuts in the Ministry of Agriculture which means there's less support for farmers. And so defunding the agricultural sector is, has really made it more complicated for farming in Costa Rica.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Can you help me to understand how that's affected you directly? So if you're receiving support from the Ministry of Agriculture, what that used to look like and what it looks like now.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: How a perfect example is Ministry of Agriculture, even as close as five years ago, would offer a series of courses and training for farmers. And two years ago there was a new administration and budget cuts came in and basically they were, the extension agents were told you can't offer any courses for farmers. And we're very lucky that we have an extension agent in the area. That said, I'm going to completely ignore that and I'm going to figure out how to get people to come give courses for farmers for free. But that is reliant on that person's initiative and that person is completely ignoring what their boss told them to. And so it makes it very difficult because if you have a problem on your farm and you require technical advice and assistance, where do you get it? And the Ministry of Agriculture is the one non biased party that could come and say, okay, you have this problem, you could fix it by A, B and C. If I reach out to any commercial place, they're going to say, yeah, buy my antibiotic, that'll fix your problem. And that's not what I want to do. So that's, that's a big challenge. Another challenge that we face is in this globalized food kind of economy that I mentioned where we're dependent on imports. And the neoliberal policies Costa Rica has, has put in place in the last 40 years that promotes a model of having food be as cheap as possible produced under a conventional model, with no thought to the nutritional value, with no thought to the environment, hidden environmental costs of producing that food. And there's very little, I guess, value placed at a consumer level of recognizing that certain types of foods are better for you, they're better for the environment, they don't have all these hidden chemical costs and environmental degradation. But you go to try to sell your product and people want it as cheap as possible. And, and the mark current market price for food doesn't take into account all those hidden subsidies, environmental subsidies, health subsidies. And, and so when, I mean right now one of the big challenges I have is I am selling my milk to the Monteverde cheese factory and I'm being placed under the same regulation aspects as anyone that's farmed conventionally. I'm getting the same price for my milk that anyone that's farmed conventionally that might have a lower cost model because they don't have that added worker labor that it's not costing them as much to produce. They're heavily reliant on Inputs on antibiotics, deworming medicines that we no longer use. And so that's a big challenge. And that is something that we're going to have to figure out as a community and as a society even. How do you educate the consumers? And how do you make people realize that our current food price structure has these hidden subsidies that are detrimental to the environment? And farmers that are doing things different need to be recognized for that price difference. We need to be paid for what we're actually doing, because otherwise it's not sustainable economically.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: So it seems that your or you're doing something a particular way, and yet your milk is going just into a regular system.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Exactly, yeah.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: You still do it, even though there are some challenges or it's not. Perhaps you're not getting fully compensated, as you expected, for it. So what motivates you to keep doing it?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Our belief system, our feeling that we have to really contribute in the aspect of climate change. But the reality is we're doing it because we're able to subsidize it with rental incomes. We have a house on Airbnb, and otherwise we wouldn't be able to. And. And the other people I know in this community that are also starting down this regenerative path, they're going to say the same thing. Oh, well, I could do it because I charge a fee to have a night tour on my farm, or I can do it because I have a house on Airbnb, because otherwise you don't cover your cost model, which is sad. You shouldn't have to rely on an external subsidy to do things. Right.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: I get it. And so do you play. Does this farm and your operations here as a model play a role in motivating others or educating others or inspiring others?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Well, yeah, I definitely see that educating others is part of our mission. One of the things we're trying to do at a community level is form a farmers association, a nonprofit, where we can start to support each other and provide some of that education and try to find markets where we have higher added value so we don't have to rely on external subsidies like an Airbnb rental to pay the light bill. So that's something that we're working at. At a community level. It's something that's in the works. It's not something that's going to have immediate results, but it's really what you have to do, because you have to be able to sell your products with that added market value, which means either I could not sell to the cheese factory, and I could say for Example produce my own cheese and market it directly. But that also has the challenges of marketing and distribution and other things. Plus a series of legal requirements for health permits and installations. And as a farmer you have, I mean at some point on this farm we're going to have to do some of that. Either produce our own coffee brand, we haven't really decided yet what, but we're going to have to do something along that lines so that we can start to sell our product with a higher added value.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: So you mentioned the formation of an association amongst farmers. Can you explain sort of what that process looks like to do that, you know, who else is potentially going to be involved and then what the advantages are to you and the other members?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Well, it's something that's in the works right now. So we're still defining at the end who exactly is going to be involved. And it's basically creating a non profit, a legal organization. So there's a law in Costa rica, the lados diagram 18 that stipulates how that is done and, and how you have to affiliate people and you constitute a legal non profit basically is what we're, what we're doing and some of our objectives, we're fine tuning them but it's basically going to be that helping farmers find added value, helping educate the consumers, trying to help support each other's finding markets and, and really and also helps perhaps achieve certifications like if you produce organic coffee, it's much easier to certify the association than it is for each member of this association to individually certify. And so those would be also benefits of establishing the association.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Yeah. Okay, so it sounds like you're not just focusing on better with this, with this vision. It sounds like there's a more so vision there.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Well, I mean you have to diversify. I mean you. Regenerative agriculture cannot be done in the context of a monoculture, whether that monoculture is dairy or coffee or whatever. Nature doesn't work in monocultures that you, you don't see them in nature. And so I mean that's an integral part of regenerative farming. So if you have a regenerative farm then you need, and you need to have multiple crops, which means you have to have market for those multiple crops. And so that's where you have to figure out how you're going to get that added value and really think at a much larger level.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: So yeah, we can step back a bit there. And you've mentioned the diversification and the products you are producing. Can we, if we can Just go into really simple terms. Just like what are you, what. What comes into the farm, what do you do it and how you. How do you do it? Produce. And then where does it go? You know that. In really simple terms.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Okay, well, in simple terms, what's going in are. Well, the financial subsidies I mentioned for the Airbnb, meat against meat. What's going in is labor, electricity, obviously, to run our milking machines and whatnot. At an animal level, we've cut out many of our inputs. We still use a few. We went from using 160, 500 pound bags of grain per month to using 30. My goal is to, over the next year, completely eliminate that as my ecosystem slowly starts to build, build back. So we've come a long way. We do have to use sometimes medicines for cows. A cow gets sick, she gets cut, you have to use medicines.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Does that change in. Have you observed a change in the health?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Yes, we have. When we started down the path of decreasing the grains, the concentrates, from 165 bags a month to 30, the animal health improves substantially. So we have had a very strong decrease in the amount of medicines we had to provide. But there still are some things. Cows get ticks. We're managing our ticks more holistically using natural funguses. We used to use chemicals. We've cut out Ivermectin. We still do have to use some antibiotics. For example, some of my cows recently got cut on their forearms and you have to apply an antibiotic like it's either that or the cow gets an infection and she dies. So. So we do have to apply some of that. I guess those are, I guess, my main inputs.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: What about the animals themselves? Do you import them or sort of vitamin.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: We have been using a system where we have a bull on site and we have been using artificial insemination. One of my big projects for this next year is shifting breeds. We have for many years, I'd say at least 40, mostly based our production on Jersey cows. Jersey cows is a European breed designed for Europe. We have, we have used them because high butterfelt fat content, high quality milk, smaller animal, which makes it fairly adaptable for the terrain, the hills and the mountains. They have less problems, for example, in the rainy season of becoming limp from. From the wet. The problem we found in recent years is being an animal that's been genetically designed for northern climates. As climate change becomes more and more of a problem, so has our susceptibility or vulnerability with these animals with ticks. And we are finding that climate change is Making some of these issues like ticks, more and more of a problem. So our decision, we made this decision six or seven months ago, is to introduce a more tropicalized breed into the herd. We're gonna work. We've been inseminating the last couple months with Senepol. Senepal is a breed that developed in the Caribbean under tropical ice conditions. They have more of a natural tick resistance, so they're not going to be as susceptible. It's also a breed that has more beef characteristics. So again, we're thinking of diversifying, not only relying on milk, but also relying on more beef breeds. That's going to allow us. Well, we have. Before we settled on Senepol, we have experimented with a couple different breeds. One of them was Charolai. In the end, we decided against it again because it was a more of a European breed. I do have one calf. The other breed that we tried was Brian Brahmin. We had problems with the birthing capability of the Jerseys. Unless I go out and sell 100 of my herd and import all Brahmins, I have to make this transition slowly by breeding using Brahmin semen. And I was having problems where it was hard for the Jerseys to give birth to a Brahmin calf because it was larger animal. So we decided against Brahmin even though it's a more tropicalized animal. We tried Angus before that. Even though Angus is a very high quality meat, we found that there was a very low resale value. Costa Rica, the market is very much designated. If you're going to sell an animal for beef, that it be Brahmin. And Senepol is in a breed that looks a lot more like Brahman, but it's not Brahman. But it looks a lot more like Brahmin than St. Angus. So what we found was we take an Angus to the market, we'd get pennies poor. And that's not the point. Right. The point is having an animal that can provide milk but be more robust, more resilient to climate change, more tropicalized for tropical diseases, but also have a good resale value because you want to be able to sell those animals that you don't want and be properly paid for them. Jerseys do not have that right now, if I sell yesterday, I sold a Jersey calf, a newborn for 5,000 colonists. The semen for that calf cost me 10,000 kronas. And so that's another reason to transition off of that breed as a milk animal. They're great, but we're not getting that resilience to climate change that we want from them.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Yeah. Okay, so let's focus just sort of close the loop on the dairy aspect. So you explained the inputs of how the process goes and then the output is milk.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Milk.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: How much milk are you producing?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: We are currently producing about. You want it? We, I guess let me figure out per day because we, when we send it, it's for every couple days. We're producing about 200 kilos a day more or less. A kilo is a liter? Yes. And milk, they're the same.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: And how does that vary throughout the.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Year in dry season and critical moments? For example, we just got through this really bad rains that we've had that haven't stopped for two months and right now it went down to, let me tell you, 157 kilos per day. So that's like a 50 kilo. Yeah, more or less. And, and so yeah, dry season it'll go down. Raising season it goes up. And that also depends on factors like just the way our farm is, how much the cow has to walk. If, if the pastures the cow uses are way at the end of the farm and I'm not giving grains as an input, then that cow is going to expend more energy walking to the end of the farm and her production is going to drop. If I could offset that by buying more grains. But then I'm shooting myself in the foot with the whole regenerative aspect. So I've made a conscious decision to do that. Before we started producing regeneratively we were producing about four times as much milk. But it was all based on grains inputs. The 165 bags of grain to 30, that's all easy energy for the cow to transform into milk. And the difference in cost is not been worth it. So now I'm actually producing less milk, but I am slightly more profitable. And that's been one of the big take homes is that you don't always have to produce more. Even though modern economics and everything else teaches us this, that's not necessarily the case.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Yeah, that's a very interesting distinction. So yeah, you talked about the profitability of the business there and you've mentioned some of the funding sources around the sort of structure. Any other notable sources of funding expensive as well?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Well, biggest expenses besides like whatever inputs, like I said, we still giving some grain, that's an important expense that runs about a thousand dollars a month. Labor, labor is a huge expense. And Costa Rican legal benefits which are important. Costa Rica has very very good worker benefits. So health care, worker Insurance, that's a very important part of our expenses. Electricity is expensive. In Costa Rica you have to have two types of insurance. You have to have general health care insurance and then you have to have a workers compensation for accident. And, and those are expensive. Those are not cheap things to have. So I would say those are some of our largest, largest expenses.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Is it profitable?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: It's not profitable. We're operating at a loss and that is something that needs to be corrected in the short term in order to be sustainable for the long term.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: What do you think the roadmap to achieve that?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Looks like we need to start having added value. And I am going to really have to start focus on marketing and finding clients that are going to be willing to pay a lot more for our products. And that's really one of my biggest challenges probably for the next six to eight months because no business is sustainable unless they're sustainable economically. You can't do it. And so that is something we're really going to have to focus on.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Do you anticipate that that's going to require the acquisition of new skills or knowledge?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Yeah, I just got back from a week long conference on regenerative agriculture in Madison and one of the main things that is, yeah, you have to learn more. And, and that's one of the cool things about regenerative agriculture is that it forces you to keep learning. And so, but, but yeah, it's. I'm gonna need to figure out what that added value is. I'm probably going to have to learn about more about marketing. I'm probably going to have to work on figuring out who my client base is going to be and who is going to be willing to pay more for food. We're lucky in that sense that we do live in a community with tourism. Hopefully there will be people that might be willing to have that pay that premium. How big is that base? How sustainable is that base? That's a big question mark. I have. I don't know. And that's going to be something we're going to have to find out.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: So that sort of teacher focus, you know, from where you've come from and the changes you've made to where you are now, like what critical skills and knowledge have you had to acquire?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: I read a lot, a lot of reading from YouTube. It's been, I've had to learn more about the soil. That's something very concrete. I've had to learn more about the ecosystem and how it works. I've had to learn a lot in terms of communicating this Knowledge to other people. When I started, everyone thought I was crazy. Like, totally crazy. And the people that visit our farm, we have students that come and university students and whatnot that come and, and, and do sort of tours occasionally. And I tell them when they come. I said, when I started, I, we, we went. We decided to cut back chemical fertilizer because of greenhouse gas emissions. And the very first reaction was from my dad, who basically said, nobody can do modern farming without chemical fertilizer. And I said, no, no, yeah, we can do it. And so then I started getting phone calls from my dad every couple days saying, sarah, I was out walking the dogs and the pastures look really terrible. You ought to consider fertilizing. And I would say, no, no, no, no, it's fine. And then I get the same phone call two or three days later. Wow. I was in the pastures in this section and have you thought about fertilizing? And this went on for a couple weeks. And one day I get a phone call that said, sarah, I bought you 50 bags of fertilizer and I want you to apply them. And I went, oh, crap, now what do I do? And so I applied one bag of fertilizer right in front of his house so he could see I did it. And I returned the other 49 bags to the ag feed supply store. And I told the guys, never, ever apply another bag of chemical fertilizer on this farm. To this day, I still frankly don't know why they listened to me and not him. Because I was the boss and he was the big boss. But they did. And I realized that I needed to rethink my strategy, that just doing changing my grazing wasn't going to be enough. And that made me think about how I was going to have to focus on soil health and learn more about it. And so I started working with some chicken manure. We did that for about two years. Thankfully, that got us over the hump. And. And then we started seeing. Within about 10 months, we started seeing the return of dung beetles out into the pastures. And so we start, instead of having manure that would sit out in the fields being toxic from the ivermectin, we started to see them decompose. Because we eliminated the ivermectin along with the chemical fertilizer, we got support from the Ministry of Agriculture that tied taught us how to make mountain microorganisms which help reintroduce soil fertility, reintroducing some of the bacteria and the fungi into the soil. That also helped us kind of reincorporate that Ministry of Agriculture training gave us that knowledge. And when we started seeing those things, then it became a little bit easier to convince people that, yeah, we didn't need chemical fertilizer. My dad came on board after about a year when he started seeing the pastures were looking a little bit better. But I still got a tremendous amount of backlash from the community, from other farmers, and people basically saying, Sarah has no idea what she's doing. Plus, I'm a woman's in the traditionally man's field and saying, no, no, she's gonna kill our cows. They're going to starve to death. People saying, no, you can't graze that way. Your cows are going to go hungry. They're going to starve. In the beginning, I would get that from the workers as well. Slowly as the workers realized that no, the cows were not starving, then they slowly became on board. But it took the Russia, Ukraine war that started two years ago, plus now it took that for the wider community to start to think about the benefits of no fertilizing that related to the cost of fertilizer. Costa rica went up 300% from one day to the next, literally over the, I don't know, a couple weeks. And I hadn't even realized it because I had quit using fertilizer for a couple years by then. But then I one day I was at a producer meeting and people are saying, oh, no, no, cost of fertilizer is 53,000 a bag. And I went, what? It used to be 10,000. And they said, yeah, and it's still rising and it's going to go up to 60, 60,000 a bag, and my farm's going to go bankrupt. And so then I started telling people, you know, you can farm without buying chemical fertilizer. And so then people are saying, oh, yeah, I heard you doing that. You want to tell me about that? And so in, in the sense that helped catalyze people starting to think differently. Now, unfortunately, the price of fertilizer has come back down and not everyone that showed interest in it is still doing it. There are a couple other people in the community that are have, that did start down the path of regenerative agriculture about two years years ago. Grete Alfredo is one farm, and Pink Maner, which is Greta Alfredo, they, they started down this path about two years ago. They've also been fairly successful at it. But again, if you look at their numbers, they're also being subsidized by tourism and by an Airbnb. But their farm has also improved this oil Health, they've increased the amount of forage they can produce. They've, they've really, they've embraced the principles of regenerative agriculture, which is exciting to see because that's something that benefits everyone. Right.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: What advice do you. Yeah, let's think about people in two camps, right? There's people who are interested already in this. What's your advice for them? And then people who are not interested at all. Would you still have. What would you want to tell them?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Oh, that's a big question. Well, the people that have embraced it. I think we need to continue strengthening the community and the sharing of information because regenerative agriculture is something that, because there is no recipes, it's different for everyone. And what works on my farm might not even work across the fence because of the manage. But there are things about it that can apply to everyone and share, sharing that information is knowledgeable because if it, if what I did doesn't work for my neighbor, maybe something my other neighbor did will be helpful for the other neighbor. Like you have to try different strategies, different ways of doing things. And so it's a process of continual learning. So people that embark down this path, I would definitely say be open to continual learning and, and thinking about your farms as an ecosystem, as a whole. People that have never even thought about going down this, I would ask them to start thinking about the sustainability of their farms if they continue down an extractive depleted model thinking about their soil health. I mean, there are statistics out there that say that globally we have only 6, 60 harvests left if we continue down a conventional model that varies in reality from country to country. Some country areas are more degraded than others. But you, you not only have to think about it from an ecological perspective, you have to think about it from a health perspective. As a society, we cannot continue to have products that are full of chemicals or full of hormones or that are detrimental to our health. Costa Rica has one of the highest pesticide herbicide uses in Latin America. And it is surprising because we have this image of a green country. But in reality our food production, if you go up on the slopes of Irasu volcano, the whole side of the volcano is bare. There's no cover crops, there's erosion. And all the production of those vegetables is all based on herbicides and chemicals, insecticides, anything that ends in I, they're probably using. And then when you learn that, you say okay, it's. Well, it's no real surprise because Rica has extraordinarily high rates of stomach cancer and other types of Cancers. I mean there's, there's a link and people have to realize there's that link and, and you can, if you just have follow a conventional profit based, volume based model with high inputs, you're ignoring those links and long term that's, that's not sustainable.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Yeah, okay, great advice. I think, you know, obviously I'm a bit biased.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Well, it's, it's a whole lot easier said than done. What I just said is probably not going to convince anybody. People have to see it for the themselves and, and they have. And that's where education and training comes in because people have to. A conventional farmer has to be able to go to a different type of farm and you have to be willing to share the numbers and you have to be willing to walk the fields and you have to be willing to look at what's there and they have to be willing to see it works.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Yeah. What, what sort of means of the transparency, transmission and knowledge sharing do you have with other people in, in doing similar things area? Like how are you communicating? How are you sharing?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Yeah, we, we have a WhatsApp group and we, we have, we're forming the Farmers association like I told you. And then in our case, we have let people come visit the farm sometime. We participate in different activities done by the Ministry of Agriculture. These training sessions that aren't supposed to be happening but are happening I was telling you about, we all go to those and it's kind of the same core group of people that are committed to that that are doing it. So.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Yeah, you mentioned the Ministry of Agriculture a few times. Obviously it plays a two different roles. Grain farmer here. What would you say their sort of perspective or relationship is with regenerative agriculture? Principle. Obviously you've received some training that's specific to regenerative agriculture from them. But big picture. What's that? What does that look like?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: On paper, the government is supporting the principles of regenerative agriculture. On paper. In reality they're not because they are defunding the Ministry of Agriculture. So in reality. Hello. Hello. How are you? Is that cream cold? Okay then I can't make the tiramisu. Just stick it in the fridge. That cream actually won't work anyway. No, it doesn't whip, huh? It depends how bad you need your tiramisu. If you can wait a day, I can pick it up this afternoon.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: We can take a break.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: That's okay. Well, just the two desserts that are on the table. That's all you need to take, I guess. Sorry. Blue parenthesis there. So in reality, yeah, they're. And so at this point it's really depending how committed the extension agents are. If the extension agents like the ones in Monteverde are committed to this, then there's quite a bit of support. There are. The Ministry of Agriculture is also undergoing a generational transformation. People that are in a more conventional mindset are slowly retiring off. And those younger people. Thank you. Yep. Those younger people are, I think, more open to some of these ideas. And so there starts to be a more acceptance of this. So there's several things happening. There's the defunding, but there's a policy on paper to support it. But how much is adopted is really depends on the extension agent themselves and the farmers obviously, and how receptive. Because you can have an extension agent that promotes it. But if they're working on the slopes of the IRU volcano, I can guarantee that they're probably not going to be very many farmers that are adopting it. Right.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Because.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Because you have to also have a receptive audience.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: This is all absolutely fascinating stuff to me. Let's just sort of. I think that's really rounded up that aspect. This is maybe quite a formulated question. I think it'll be useful for us to understand, to do a SWOT analysis of strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, threats for the fun. And you mentioned some of these, of course. But you know, in simple terms, you know, where are you strongest as a, as an operation, where are you weakest?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Well, I would say we're strongest in our capability and knowledge on milk production because that's obviously what we've done the longest and that's what we know about. Our weaknesses are definitely in the marketing and creating added value proposals for our products. That's definitely a weakness right now. There's a lot of opportunity in that area. And threats, I would say definitely the long term sustainability based on the economic threat. Like if, if let's go down a rabbit hole and say if we cannot develop a market base that's willing to pay for that added value, it's not going to be viable. It's as simple as that. So can I find people that are willing to pay more for whatever our main product ends up being? I mean, we're transitioning away from milk being our main product to we're diversifying into beef, we're diversifying into garden things. But if I sell some cilantro and say my roll of cilantro costs a thousand and everyone else at the farmer's market cost 500, if no one buys it, then it's not sustainable. So I would say that's one of the biggest threats that we have. So there's a huge opportunity as we diversify into trying to figure out what are potential markets. But if you don't have the clients that are willing to pay for it, it's not going to go very far.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Sounds like a puzzle.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: It is kind of a puzzle, yeah.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: How would you define success? What would the successful future look like to you?

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Well, our goal and what we're working towards has success at several levels. For us, obviously achieving financial success, we're not going to get rich, but we do have to cover the bills. That's success at one level, definitely. But also one of our main goals is really, really making our farm more resilient to climate change. And for us, that is success. So achieving that regeneration of the soil, that ecological diversity, that capacity to be more drought resistant, be more resistant to extreme weather events. May they be wind, may they be rain. Like this past November is the wettest November that we've ever had on record. Monteverde got more than a thousand millimeters of rain. Rain, yeah, we all felt it, right. It didn't stop raining for in November. I mean, that's never happened in November. And, and you, you need to be able to have the capability to be resilient to that. And, and you need to have the soil structure so that you can absorb that moisture, so that come March, some of that moisture is still in my soil. So. So for us, success is, is at many levels going to be resilience to climate change. That's one of the main drivers that started us down this road, is lowering our greenhouse gas emissions, contributing as much as possible to this. And for us, that's really important because, I mean, I've been a climate change activist for about 12 years and I give talks about it, I've studied it. People don't realize that climate change is not a linear, it is exponential. And at the beginning it seems like it's linear, but we're getting to the point now we've passed or passed 1.5 degrees warming. The Paris Agreement is almost going to be impossible to achieve. Now we're coming to the point where we're going to get to these exponential changes and, and we're starting to see them like there's some hints out there, these tropical storms that break all the records. I mean, Helene was just one of them. But you start to see more and more hints of what's going to come. But most people don't realize that. And you need to really have an ecosystem that can be as resilient as possible. I mean one of the things we're doing is planting lowland fruit trees and we're trying to. We know that what's going to happen in Monteverde, Monteverde is going to have a climate like Guanacaste in our lifetimes. And so how do we now take steps to start to make our farm more resilient, to be able to deal with these climate adjustments and still be able to have an ecosystem that can exist at some level?

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Yeah, I think if we can localize it so within, within the data it'd be very useful to get the context of what, where Monteverde is, what we can, what it's, what the situation is here, the context and what's changing over time.

entries.speaker: Sarah
entries.text: Oh yeah. Well, Monteverde is located, our farm ranges from 1350 meters to 1,420. So we're about 4,000ft more or less if you think in feet. We have a rainy season and a dry season. Monteverdi gets about, give or take, anywhere from 2 to 3 meters of rain a year. We got a meter of rain just in November. So that's a perfect example of how things are changing. We're getting, we have had very clear documented changes in our weather patterns. Between 1960 and 1970, Monteverde had an average of 25 dry days a year. Now we have anywhere from 110 to 115 dry days a year. But it is raining more. So what's happening is the weather patterns are changing. That weather changing weather pattern happens means like for example, we'll get dry days and you've seen it day after day, beautiful weather and then boom, we get an extreme rainstorm within a couple hours. Where, where you get the whole month's rain or 3 weeks rain or 2 months rain or whatever in a short amount of time. That generates erosion, runoff, problems like what we experienced on September 23rd where we had extreme flooding where the road almost washed out. We're getting altitudinal migration of species. For example, the fer de lance snake snakes moving up the mountain. We are seeing it in the birds, the snakes. We're seeing changes in animal distribution. For example, the magpie J never used to be here two, three years ago. It became a permanent resident right here at my house. At your house too. But now you start to see them further up. One of the things we're contributing with on this farm is supporting long term research. Ricky Winden and Alan Pounds are doing a very long 30 year study to document the changes in animal species that's been ongoing, gee, gosh, five or eight years now, I don't remember a long time. But just in that short amount of time, Ricky is documented very strong changes in population of animals. That's a very strong indicator. We're also seeing more extreme heat. So temperatures are going up. So in that context, how do we make our farm more resilient? I mean, one very small example is planting mango trees. Mangoes are low land, fruit shouldn't grow here. It's growing, it's doing great, which is exciting, but it's also very scary because it shouldn't be growing. So how do you make your ecosystem as resilient as possible to deal with those changes? How can we try to predict what's going to happen? How do we try to have an impact? How do we. A positive impact? Because there could be negative impacts too. But how do we really try to, to make, to create an ecosystem that can at least withstand the worst of those impacts. And that's a huge goal that's going to take the rest of my life and probably most of my daughter's life as well, because that's an ongoing thing and it's going to be harder and harder to do as the effects of climate change get more extreme. So when you ask me to define success, that's one of our long term goals, which is pretty ambitious, but it's also necessary. I mean, we're always going to need food as a community, as a society, as a planet. And if we don't make our farms more resilient, if we don't embrace regenerative agriculture, we're not going to have that. And then what do we do? I mean, as a society, how do we, how do we deal with these issues? Well, those are some pretty big questions.

entries.speaker: Finn

entries.text: Of course. Well, obviously the owners of the land, the family, I guess you could say the ecosystem at some level is a stakeholder because, I mean, that's what we're trying to build back. And that's what we're trying to. To help the workers, the clients, I think, at some level, the community, because you have to have local food systems. And that's something that Costa Rica is losing. And so at many levels, at a community level, people need to be aware of our food production systems, and they need to be aware of what we can produce and consume locally. And. And I guess Covid brought that to highlight a little bit. When Costa Rica shut down and people didn't have jobs, and they said, well, we'll go back and garden. And then they realized that the knowledge of how to garden was lost. People didn't know how to garden. Well, my grandpa used to do it, but that was grandpa. Right. So I think community systems are also stakeholders, and that makes us all a stakeholder in our agricultural systems because we all depend on them at some level. And so. And we need to support them. I mean, something that frustrates me a little bit is on some of the WhatsApp community chats, oftentimes the cheese factory is slammed. People say, oh, well, the cheese factory. Well, the pipeline broke and there was viewing water into the river and the. And you see people put things like, well, we need to get this transnational out of the community. Well, that transnational is buying milk from 125 local families. And if that factory isn't here, those local families, there's not enough tourism for them to all be able to diversify into tourism. There's not an alternative market. Some of them could probably transition to beef. Some of them could maybe build, urbanize their farms. But does that really serve the community? And when I see people put things on that, I try to highlight, like, no, there are people that depend on this food production. And oftentimes people on these social chats, I mean, on social media, people post anything and everything, invent their frustrations. But people don't see that. And they're like, they just see, well, it's. It's a factory in the middle of my community, and. And I moved here five years ago, and I want to live a nice rural life. Well, yeah, but there are people that depend socioeconomically on that cheese factory. Is it the best production model? Maybe not. Can we get the farmers that are providing that milk to the factory all engaged in regenerative practices, then all of a sudden, that would really push that to be more sustainable. And so I think people need to be aware at a community level of what their food production systems are. So that's a really long answer, but I think that is important. And it's the same for coffee. We're lucky that most of our coffee production here is relatively small. People have independent brands they can market directly to tourism with added value. That's harder with the milk and dairy industry. So there's less pushback. But if one of those farms were to become larger and larger and become more conventional, they'd probably get that same negative pushback from the community.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Yeah, it's an adjusting community for so many reasons. But we don't get into that. I think we've got some good contacts of the community here which is useful for the research. Of course, one very, very small detail which just, we can just sort of look back in a practical sense, you're supplying your milk to the juice factory. How often does that happen? How often do you get the day.

entries.text: It happens anywhere from every two to every four days they send out a tank, truck down, a bulk tank down to pick it up. So they do constant monitoring of quality and if we meet 100% of their quality stipulations, which includes bacteria, somatic cells, solid content, protein content, back content and say we hit top of the charts on everything. Our price is about 395 kilometers per kilo. If, but that's variable. Like if, if one cow gets mastitis and my somatic cell count goes up, even just one day, I. My price could be knocked down to 390 or 385 or, or let's say my pasture change changes and I transition from dry season to rainy season, the protein content of my pasture changes, which then affects the protein and fat content of my milk. Boom. My price can go down. And so the pr. The price is never the same. There's a sliding scale based on those variables. No, and please feel, I mean, if you want more information, I'm happy to provide it.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Honestly, I think we've really covered everything really nicely. You've talked very clearly and very comprehensively. Great knowledge and experience which I've really enjoyed. And the last thing to say. Is there anything that you would like, off the record, that you've shared just in case there's anything on your mind?

So I think it's important that we talk about these things in an open manner and, and, and people become aware of the challenges and the difficulties and. Because that's the only way we can fix them. I mean, it's the same with climate change. When I give my talks about climate change, I am very blunt about what's going to happen. I back it up with science, but I tell people exactly this is what's going to happen. And oftentimes people have a deer in headlights look. And I say, look, we cannot fix it it if we don't really understand what's broken. And it's the same with regenerative agriculture. We cannot fix a broken system if we don't know how it's broken. And, and so talking about the issues and the problems and the challenges is really the only way we're going to be able to, to, to change the system. And it, yes, it all starts with the biology of the soil. Yes, it all starts with the diversity of the farm, with your organic matter and your carbon contents and, and your fertility of the soil. But we can't fix that unless we realize that our current conventional model is broken. You're very welcome.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Absolutely fantastic.

entries.speaker: Sarah
