entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Okay, so, Finn, hello. Thank you for joining us. Today we're going to be debriefing your first case in Costa Rica. So please let us know first of all the name of the initiative and then how was the process of contacting them. And then you can just briefly describe what the case was about and all the things you find most relevant about it. Okay. You take your time as much as you want.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: Cool. Okay. So the case was Finca Laguna Verde, which is a regenerative rotational dairy grazing and milking operation. They were very easy to contact because they're my neighbors. They're about 100 meters from my desk here, right next door. And that's why I chose them as a first case, because they're very accessible to me. The coordinator's bilingual. She was born here, but two American parents and so speaks English. And so for my first case I wanted to do the first couple in English. Obviously at some point I'll be doing them in Spanish while I'm here in Costa Rica, but just to get familiar with the protocols, wanted to do the first couple in English. She was easy to coordinate with. We had WhatsApp and we just went back and forth a bit. She was enthusiastic and forthcoming. In fact, I had them on my hit list. But she reached out to me. I put a post in some local groups saying if anybody is doing any of this kind of stuff or is interested or knows other projects, let me know. And she reached out straight away and said, we can help you. We're doing regenerative agriculture here. What do you need basically? So I gave her the basic brief introduction for the the process, what it's going to look like, managing expectations and understanding the time and resource that is required for it. She was pretty forthcoming. But people are busy, so finding a good four hour chunk of the morning or an afternoon can be challenging for some cases. I think we managed to do it, I think in three hours, but that's because it's very close and it's quite familiar. I think that saved time and we had rapport already. We didn't need to do as much of that because we know each other, so that helped. It took a few weeks to find time for it, which I think is going to be a common occurrence when people are busy. But we got there in the end, so I went to take a visit and I've already seen the farm, so we sort of skipped the initial tour because I'm familiar with it. Went straight into the interview, which was also requirement request from her because she had some other stuff Going on again, I feel like because of the repo we already have, that was okay to do that. But in the future I probably would try to use that opportunity to build that relationship. First, the interview went really great. Oh, so just a brief sort of background to the farm, to the project. So it's a dairy farm that's been here for about 50 years, operating here for about 50 years. Her family bought it 30 or 40 years ago, in the 80s I think, and she was raised here. And then in recent years, I think about seven or eight years ago, her father sort of aged out of wanting to run the farm and handed over responsibility to her and she immediately went on to try and change the practices for the better, basically to go regenerative. I think she received some training from the Ministry of Agriculture here and got support from peers who are doing similar things, resources online and things, and started putting things into work. She received a lot of backlash from the local community, other farmers, even from her own father saying, you know, you're crazy, this is a bad idea. You're going to kill the cows, it's not going to work, you don't know what you're doing. She received a lot of, really a lot of pushback and she just credit to her, she just plowed on steady knowing that it would take time to transition. And since then it's proven itself. There are so many positive outcomes for the farm, for the health of the cows, for the health of the land that sort of prove how this is a viable way to go and that it's a good idea for some people. And now they've become a resource. People come to them to get advice. So it's a real turnaround that she was sort of maligned in the beginning and now is considered a leader in this field. It's fairly small farm, small herd, small dairy, 60 something cows. They milk around 30 at any given time. Take a few hundred liters a day and I think by now it's working really well. She said it took a few years for the transition to really catch up, for the health of the land to catch up, for the productivity to rebound, to find the right balance with the inputs and the ship changing sort of dynamic I think took time to calibrate but it seems like by now they've got a pretty fluid and sort of, well, sort of a good track record. Things are running quite well. She does note that it requires a lot more labor and observation to do it this way than the old ways. You've really got to be paying really close attention to the cows, to the land, to the weather, to the growth of the grass, to the condition of the land. But the extra labor costs that go into this are traded off against the reduction in their input costs, which is feed and medication, which they need much less of now because the cows are much healthier. And so it sort of balances out and it's actually marginally more profitable now than it used to be. But they're basically still breaking even from a sort of financial perspective, which is the case for dairying in Costa Rica. Almost all dairy farms break even. There are subsidies and government systems that make it viable, but only that's what makes it viable. Otherwise it's not super lucrative necessarily, and they seem to be doing it really well. She's extremely knowledgeable. She spoke very confidently and very in great detail and really seems to know her stuff. And so she was a great first case that really could give, I thought, good quality data. And she was a very cooperative interviewee. She was really gracious. It wasn't like pulling teeth trying to get data from her. She was pretty forthcoming. So that was a nice sort of first start as well for me. Um, I think that's about, you know, a brief introduction of the. Of the case. If you have any questions, go ahead.

And not just climate change, but respect for the land, the health of the land has drastically transformed since they've changed their practices. And she I think could see it was clear that she just, she was motivated to seeing the degraded land was saddening to her. And to do something about that was just, I think just the right thing to do. From her perspective, this is what we should be doing. We'll be doing what we can. And then in this context, we're feeling the effects of climate change because of our elevational gradient band of certain climate and conditions moving up the mountain year by year or decade by decade. And it's on its up the mountain and it's changing. And so they want to be able to prepare for the future as well. But it goes hand in hand by restoring the land into good health. It's also more resilient to climate change. So she was pretty clear that both of these things are a win for them and that's what motivates them really. And then the second part of the question was vision for the future.

entries.speaker: Finn

I think that's the vision here and to keep doing it and to keep contributing to the body of work and the body of knowledge about this. So they train people, they are kind of figureheads in the community for this form of agriculture. And they've taken on or given access to their land to some local scientists who are doing sort of long term studies documenting biodiversity, species change. What else are they recording? I think those are the main things, but they're recording the change in species and diversity as indicators for climate change. So species moving in or moving out are indicators for the movement of climate change at the mountain. entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: That's amazing. This kind of educational endeavor is just more like a market clustering, partnering thing.

You know, it's, it's going to be a self selected group of course, who bring this in. The bit surprised me most was learning that the Ministry, Ministry of Agriculture has, has been training on this. This isn't her directly, but the local extension agent was essentially. How does it go exactly? Something like he was directed by his superiors to stop serving this area basically and he's just gone rogue. Did she mention anything related to that?

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo

But they are still making the same amount of money, basically. She's accepted that they will produce less milk, but they're spending less on inputs to make the milk. And it comes out fairly even. She said it's very small difference between the two, even though the quantity of milk being produced is vastly lower. So there's that as part of the answer. And then all the milk goes to a local cheese factory which was established by the community a long time ago and run by the community and just within the last, I think, five years, was bought by a sort of large international conglomerate dairy whatever. And they monitor every. I can't remember all of the different sort of factors of the milk that they monitor, but it's things like indicators for health. So it wouldn't be viruses, but some sort of indicator for bacteria. Well, that's one of them for sure, you know, so bacterial load, but it's something else. There's some, something that is produced in the milk when there's certain health problems with the cows. Um, they measure it for fat content, protein content, a bunch of other stuff. She, she reeled them off a bunch of times. And I don't know about dairy. And she says most of the time hers is at the top. You know, it's taking all the box. It's, you know, they have a sort of grading scale for the quality of the milk based on the amount of these different things in the milk. And most of the time hers are at the top or near the top. And so there's some of the higher quality milk and they get a very small premium for the quality of the milk. And that's not because of the regenerative source, but it's just because the quality of the milk on these. But it's a fraction of a difference of one or two colones, which is a tiny, tiny fraction of a center for, you know, a few percent difference in these different markers of the quality.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: That's great. That's great. That's what I. That's kind of what I thought. We, we do have some cases elsewhere, so I just came from visiting a dairy farm as well, and we discussed a lot of that is different because it's. It's not the same kind of approach, but in none we do have a very similar one. And 100 to grass fed, his production really dropped, but then the quality, you know, really, really went up and he then got a chance to do some other stuff with that milk. So that's great to know.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: The biggest one was the health of the cows is one of the biggest differences, so.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Yes, exactly. Like investment in, in vaccination or in antibiotics and things like that, which also affects the quality of the milk in the long term.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Finn
entries.text: Good. Okay, so maybe my last couple of questions would be around stakeholders. So first, has you felt any pushback from former providers, you know, like other concentrate providers or things like that, or medicine providers or things like that? We've seen also some trend there that they really don't want these farms to really thrive because then other people start kind of migrating to the model and of course they're losing part of their game. So I don't know if she mentioned any of that or how the provider scheme has changed a bit.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: Right. That's a really good question, and it's not one that I thought to ask. And it's not one that she mentioned anything about. entries.speaker: Finn
